raw_question_text: A d.c. electromagnet is to be constructed by winding a coil of $N$ turns tightly on an iron yoke shaped like a doughnut with a small slab sliced out to form the gap as in Fig. 2.34. The radii for the doughnut are $a$ and $b$ and the width of the gap is $W$. The permeability $\mu$ for the iron can be assumed constant and large. A wire of radius $r$ and resistivity $\rho$ is to be used for the coil. The completed magnet will be operated by placing the coil across a d.c. power supply of voltage $V$. For simplicity, assume that $b/a \gg 1$ and $a/r \gg 1$. Derive expressions for the following quantities: The steady state value for the magnetic field in the gap.

extraction_notes: image field contains a PIL image object string rather than a file path or URL
metadata.reasoning_chain: In the steady state, as $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{B}=0$ and the cross section of the iron yoke is the same everywhere, $B$ must be a constant in the yoke. Applying $\oint \mathbf{H} \cdot d \mathbf{l}=NI$ to the doughnut, we have $\frac{B}{\mu}(2\pi b-W)+\frac{B}{\mu_{0}}W=NI$ giving $B=\frac{NI\mu_{0}\mu}{\mu_{0}(2\pi b-W)+\mu W}\approx\frac{NI\mu_{0}\mu}{\mu_{0}2\pi b+\mu W}$. As $I=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{V}{\rho\frac{N2\pi a}{\pi r^{2}}}=\frac{Vr^{2}}{2a\rho N}$, the steady state value for the magnetic field in the gap is $B=\frac{\mu_{0}\mu V r^{2}}{2a\rho(2\pi b\mu_{0}+W\mu)}$.

raw_question_text: A circular wire of radius $R$ carries a current $i$ electromagnetic units. A sphere of radius $a$ ($a \ll R$) made of paramagnetic material with permeability $\mu$ is placed with its center at the center of the circuit. Determine the force per unit area on the sphere.

extraction_notes: images field contains only a PIL image object string, not a concrete file path or URL

metadata.reasoning_chain: The surface current density on the sphere is given by the boundary condition $\alpha_{m}=\mathbf{n}\times(\mathbf{H}_{2}-\mathbf{H}_{1})$. With $\mathbf{H}_{1}=\frac{\mathbf{B}_{1}}{\mu_{0}}$ and $\mathbf{H}_{2}=\frac{\mathbf{B}_{2}}{\mu}-\mathbf{M}$, and using the continuity condition $\mathbf{n}\times(\mathbf{B}_{2}-\mathbf{B}_{1})=0$, one finds $\alpha_{M}=\mathbf{M}\times\mathbf{n}=\frac{3(\mu-\mu_{0}) i}{2(\mu+2\mu_{0})R}\sin\theta\,\mathbf{e}_{\varphi}$. Then the force per unit area on the sphere is obtained by $\mathbf{f}=\alpha_{M}\times\mathbf{B}_{0}$. Substituting $\mathbf{B}_{0}=\frac{\mu_{0} i}{2R}\mathbf{e}_{z}$ and evaluating the cross product, we arrive at $\mathbf{f}=\frac{3\mu_{0}(\mu-\mu_{0}) i^{2}}{4(\mu+2\mu_{0})R}\sin\theta\left(\cos\theta\,\mathbf{e}_{\theta}+\sin\theta\,\mathbf{e}_{r}\right)$.

raw_question_text: In Fig. 2.56 an iron needle 1 cm long and 0.1 cm in diameter is placed in a uniform magnetic field of $H_{0}=1000 \mathrm{Gs}$ with its long axis along the field direction. Give an approximate formula for $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{r})$ valid for distances $r \gg 1 \mathrm{~cm}$. Here $r$ is measured from the center of the needle as origin. (Note: the saturation value of $\mathbf{B}$ in iron is approximately 2000 Gs.)

metadata.reasoning_chain: For distances $r \gg 1 \mathrm{~cm}$ the iron needle can be treated as a magnetic dipole with moment $\mathbf{m}$. Take the $z$-axis along the axis of the needle. Write $\mathbf{H}_{\text{ext}}=H_{0}\mathbf{e}_{z}$, $\mathbf{m}=m\mathbf{e}_{z}$. In Gaussian units the magnetic field at position $\mathbf{r}$ is approximately $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{r})=\mathbf{H}_{0}-\nabla\left(\frac{\mathbf{m}\cdot\mathbf{r}}{r^{3}}\right)=\mathbf{H}_{0}-\frac{\mathbf{m}}{r^{3}}+\frac{3(\mathbf{m}\cdot\mathbf{r})\mathbf{r}}{r^{5}}$. As the magnetic field due to the iron needle is much weaker than the external field $H_{0}$, $m/r^{3}\ll H_{0}$. As the tangential component of $\mathbf{H}$ is continuous at the boundary, the magnetic field within the iron needle may be taken as approximately $\mathbf{H}_{\mathrm{in}}=\mathbf{H}_{0}$. The magnetization of the needle is then $\mathbf{M}=\frac{1}{4\pi}\left(\mathbf{B}_{\text{in}}-\mathbf{H}_{\text{in}}\right)$. With the volume of the needle equal to $V=(0.05)^{2}\pi\,\mathrm{cm}^{3}$, $B_{\text{in}}=2\times10^{4}\mathrm{Gs}$, $H_{\text{in}}\approx H_{0}=10^{3}\mathrm{Gs}$, the magnetic moment of the needle is $m=V\,M=\frac{(0.05)^{2}\pi}{4\pi}\times(2-0.1)\times10^{4}=11.9\,\mathrm{Gs}\cdot\mathrm{cm}^{3}$. In polar coordinates the magnetic field at distances $r \gg 1 \mathrm{~cm}$ has components $H_{r}=H_{0}\cos\theta+2m\frac{\cos\theta}{r^{3}}=(1000+\frac{23.8}{r^{3}})\cos\theta\,\mathrm{Gs}$ and $H_{\theta}=-H_{0}\cos\theta+\frac{m\sin\theta}{r^{3}}=(-1000+\frac{11.9}{r^{3}})\sin\theta\,\mathrm{Gs}$.

raw_question_text: A long solenoid of radius $b$ and length $l$ is wound so that the axial magnetic field is $$\mathbf{B}=\begin{cases} B_{0}\,\mathbf{e}_{z}, & r<b,\\ 0, & r>b, \end{cases}$$ A particle of charge $q$ is emitted with velocity $v$ perpendicular to a central rod of radius $a$ (see Fig. 2.59). The electric force on the particle is given by $qE=f(r)\,\mathbf{e}_{r}$, where $\mathbf{e}_{r}\cdot\mathbf{e}_{z}=0$. We assume $\mathbf{v}$ is sufficiently large so that the particle passes out of the solenoid and does so without hitting the solenoid. Find the angular momentum of the particle about the axis of the solenoid, for $r>b$.

extraction_notes: image field contains a PIL object string representation rather than a concrete file path or URL
question references Fig. 2.59, but the record marks vision relevance as optional
metadata.reasoning_chain: As $\mathbf{v}$ is very large, we can consider any deviation from a straight line path to be quite small in the emitting process. Let $v_{\perp}$ be the transverse velocity of the particle. We have $m\frac{d\mathbf{v}_{\perp}}{dt}=q\,\mathbf{v}\times\mathbf{B}$. As $\mathbf{v}\perp\mathbf{B}$, $dv_{\perp}=\frac{q}{m}B_{0}vd t=\frac{q}{m}B_{0}dr$ and $$v_{\perp}(b)=\int_{a}^{b}\frac{q}{m}B_{0}\,dr=\frac{q}{m}B_{0}(b-a).$$ At $r=b$ the angular momentum of the particle about the axis of the solenoid has magnitude $$|\mathbf{r}\times m\mathbf{v}_{\perp}(b)|_{r=b}=mb\,v_{\perp}(b)=qB_{0}b(b-a)$$ and direction $-\mathbf{e}_{z}$. Thus the angular momentum is $$\mathbf{J}_{p}=-qB_{0}b(b-a)\,\mathbf{e}_{z}.$$ For $r>b$, $\mathbf{B}=0$ and $\mathbf{J}_{p}$ is considered. So $\mathbf{J}_{p}$ is the angular momentum of the particle about the axis for $r>b$.

raw_question_text: A model for an electron consists of a shell of charge distributed uniformly on the surface of a sphere of radius $a$. The electron moves with velocity $v\ll c$. Use the value of $a$ to calculate the energy in the field of the moving charge and compare it with the rest-mass energy and kinetic energy.

extraction_notes: image field contains a PIL object representation rather than a concrete file path or URL
metadata.reasoning_chain: The energy in the electromagnetic field is obtained from $W=\int \frac{1}{8\pi}(E^2+B^2)dV$. Inserting $E=\frac{e}{r^2}$ and $B=\frac{ev\sin\theta}{cr^2}$, and integrating over space (with $r$ from $a$ to $\infty$ and over angular variables), one finds $W=\frac{e^2}{2a}\left(1+\frac{2}{3}\frac{v^2}{c^2}\right)$. Recognizing that $\frac{e^2}{2a}=\frac{3}{4}mc^2$, this result can be written as $W=\frac{3}{4}mc^2+\frac{1}{2}mv^2$, so that for $v\ll c$, the kinetic energy contribution is much smaller than the rest energy.

raw_question_text: In the electrical circuit shown in Fig. 3.36, $\omega$, $R_{1}$, $R_{2}$ and $L$ are fixed; $C$ and $M$ (the mutual inductance between the identical inductors $L$) can be varied. Find values of $M$ and $C$ which maximize the power dissipated in resistor $R_{2}$. What is the maximum power? You may assume, if needed, $R_{2}>R_{1}, \omega L / R_{2}>10$.

metadata.reasoning_chain: Assuming that the primary and secondary currents are directed as in Fig. 3.36, we have the circuit equations $\dot{V}_{0} = R_{1}\dot{I}_{1} + \dot{I}_{1}\left[\frac{1}{j\omega C} + j\omega L\right] + j\omega M\dot{I}_{2}$ and $0 = \dot{I}_{2}R_{2} + j\omega L\dot{I}_{2} + j\omega M\dot{I}_{1}$. The above simultaneous equations have solution $I_{2} = \frac{j\omega M C V_{0}}{C\left[\omega^{2}(L^{2}-M^{2}) - R_{1}R_{2}\right] - L + j\left[\frac{R_{2}}{\omega} - \omega L C(R_{1}+R_{2})\right]}$. As $P_{2} = \frac{1}{2}|I_{2}|^{2}R_{2}$, when $|I_{2}|$ is maximized $P_{2}$ is maximized also. We have $|I_{2}| = \frac{\omega V_{0}}{\left\{\left[\frac{1}{M}\left(\omega^{2}L^{2} - R_{1}R_{2} - \frac{L}{C}\right) - \omega^{2}M\right]^{2} + \left[\frac{1}{MC}\left(\frac{R_{2}}{\omega} - \omega L C(R_{1}+R_{2})\right)\right]^{2}\right\}^{\frac{3}{2}}}$. As the numerator is fixed and the denominator is the square root of the sum of two squared terms, when the two squared terms are minimum at the same time $|I_{2}|$ will achieve its maximum. The minimum of the second squared term is zero, for which we require $C = \frac{R_{2}}{\omega^{2}L(R_{1}+R_{2})}$, giving $|I_{2}| = \frac{\omega V_{0}}{\omega^{2}M + \frac{R_{1}R_{2} + \frac{R_{1}\omega^{2}L^{2}}{R_{2}}}{M}}$. Minimizing the above denominator, we require $M = \sqrt{\frac{R_{1}R_{2}}{\omega^{2}} + \frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}L^{2}}$. Hence, for $M = \sqrt{\frac{R_{1}R_{2}}{\omega^{2}} + \frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}L^{2}}$, $C = \frac{R_{2}}{\omega^{2}L(R_{1}+R_{2})}$, $P_{2}$ is maximum, having the value $P_{2} = \frac{1}{2}|I_{2}|^{2}R_{2} = \frac{V_{0}^{2}}{8R_{1}\left(1+\frac{\omega^{2}L^{2}}{R_{2}^{2}}\right)}$. Supposing $\omega L / R_{2} > 10$, we obtain $P_{2} = \frac{V_{0}^{2}R_{2}^{2}}{8\omega^{2}L^{2}R_{1}}$ as the maximum power dissipated in $R_{2}$.

$$
with

$$

Integrating the circuit equation we obtain
$$
q_{1}=\int_{0}^{\infty}  rac{M}{R_{1}} d i_{2}+\int_{0}^{\infty}  rac{L_{1}}{R_{1}} d i_{1}= rac{M}{R_{1}} i_{2}(\infty) .
$$

When the coil is moved into the magnetic field $\mathbf{B}_{2}$, its induced emf is
$$
\varepsilon_{2}=- rac{d \psi}{d t}
$$
with
$$
\psi_{1}(\infty)=-N B_{2} S, \quad \psi_{1}(0)=0 .
$$

Thus
$$
 rac{d q_{2}}{d t}=i_{2}= rac{\varepsilon_{2}}{R_{1}}= rac{-1}{R_{1}}  rac{d \psi_{1}}{d t}
$$
giving
$$
q_{2}= rac{N B_{2} \pi a^{2}}{R_{1}} .
$$

As $q \propto 	heta$, we have
$$

or

$$

raw_question_text: The square-box in Fig. 3.71 represents an unknown linear lumpedconstant passive network. The source of emf at the left is assumed to have zero internal impedance. It is known that if the input emf $e_{i}(t)$ is a step function, i.e., $e_{i}(t)=\begin{cases}0 & t\leq 0\\ A & t>0\end{cases}$ then the open-circuit (no-load) output voltage $e_{0}(t)$ is of the form $e_{0}(t)=\begin{cases}0, & t\leq 0\\ \frac{1}{2}A[1-\exp(-t/\tau)], & t>0\end{cases}$ where the constant $\tau$ has the value $\tau=1.2\times10^{-4} \;\mathrm{s}$. Find the open-circuit (no load) output voltage $e_{0}(t)$ when the input is given by $e_{i}(t)=4\cos(\omega t)$ volts where $\omega$ corresponds to the frequency 1500 cycles/sec.

metadata.reasoning_chain: We first use the Laplace transform to find the transmission function $H(s)$ of the network in the frequency domain. The Laplace transform of the equation $e_{i}(t)=A \cdot U(t)$ is $E_{i}(s)=A/S$. Similarly, the Laplace transform of the output $e_{0}(t)$ is $E_{0}(s)=\frac{1}{2}A\left[\frac{1}{S}-\frac{1}{S+1/\tau}\right]$. Hence the transmission function is $H(s)=\frac{E_{0}(s)}{E_{i}(s)}=\frac{\frac{1}{2\tau}}{s+\frac{1}{\tau}}$. The Laplace transform of the new input $e_{i}(t)=4\cos(\omega t)$ is $E_{i}(s)=\frac{4s}{\omega^{2}+s^{2}}$ giving the output as $E_{0}(s)=E_{i}(s)\cdot H(s)=\frac{4s}{\omega^{2}+s^{2}}\times\frac{\frac{1}{2\tau}}{s+\frac{1}{\tau}}=\frac{2}{\tau}\left[\frac{1}{s+\frac{1}{\tau}}\cdot\frac{-\frac{1}{\tau}}{\omega^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{\tau}\right)^{2}}+\frac{\frac{1}{2}}{s-i\omega}\times\frac{1}{i\omega+\frac{1}{\tau}}+\frac{\frac{1}{2}}{s+i\omega}\times\frac{1}{i\omega+\frac{1}{\tau}}\right]$, where $\omega\tau=2\pi\times1500\times1.2\times10^{-4}\approx1$. The reverse transformation of $E_{0}(s)$ gives the open-circuit output voltage $V_{0}(t)=\frac{2}{\tau}\left[\frac{-\frac{1}{\tau}}{\omega^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{\tau}\right)^{2}}e^{-t/\tau}+\frac{\frac{1}{\tau}\cos(\omega t)+\omega\sin(\omega t)}{\omega^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{\tau}\right)^{2}}\right]\simeq-e^{-t/\tau}+\cos(\omega t)+\sin(\omega t)$

raw_question_text: The pions that are produced when protons strike the target at Fermilab are not all moving parallel to the initial proton beam. A focusing device, called a "horn", (actually two of them are used as a pair) is used to deflect the pions so as to cause them to move more closely towards the proton beam direction. This device consists of an inner cylindrical conductor along which a current flows in one direction and an outer cylindrical conductor along which the current returns. Between these two surfaces there is produced a toroidal magnetic field that deflects the mesons that pass through this region. At first, calculate the approximate inductance of this horn using the dimensions shown in the figure. The current of charged pions and protons is negligible compared with the current in the conductors.

metadata.reasoning_chain: The magnetic induction at a point between the cylinders distance $r$ from the axis is in the $\mathbf{e}_{\theta}$ direction and has magnitude $B=\frac{\mu_{0}I}{2\pi r}$. The magnetic flux crossing a longitudinal cross section of a unit length of the horn is $\phi=2\int_{0.05}^{0.4}B\,dr \approx \frac{\mu_{0}I}{\pi}\int_{0.05}^{0.4}\frac{1}{r}\,dr = 8.3\times10^{-7}\cdot I$. Hence the inductance is approximately $L=\frac{\phi}{I} \approx 8.3\times10^{-7}\,\mathrm{H}$.

raw_question_text: Two electric dipole oscillators vibrate with the same frequency $\omega$, but their phases differ by $\frac{\pi}{2}$. The amplitudes of the dipole moments are both equal to $\mathbf{P}_{0}$, but the two vectors are at an angle $\psi_{0}$ to each other, (let $\mathbf{P}_{1}$ be along the $x$-axis and $\mathbf{P}_{2}$ in the $xy$ plane) as in Fig. 4.36. For an oscillating dipole $\mathbf{P}$ at the origin, the $\mathbf{B}$-field in the radiation zone is given by $\mathbf{B}=k^{2} \frac{1}{r} e^{i k r}\left(\frac{\mathbf{r}}{r} \times \mathbf{P}\right)$. The dipole moment of the whole system is $\mathbf{P}=\mathbf{P}_{1}+\mathbf{P}_{2}$ and the magnetic field in Gaussian units is given by $\mathbf{B}=k^{2} \frac{1}{r} e^{i k r}(\mathbf{e}_{r} \times \mathbf{P})$. Using $\mathbf{e}_{x}=\mathbf{e}_{r}\sin\theta\cos\varphi+\mathbf{e}_{\theta}\cos\theta\cos\varphi-\mathbf{e}_{\varphi}\sin\varphi$, $\mathbf{e}_{y}=\mathbf{e}_{r}\sin\theta\sin\varphi+\mathbf{e}_{\theta}\cos\theta\sin\varphi+\mathbf{e}_{\varphi}\cos\varphi$, and $e^{i\frac{\pi}{2}}=i$, we obtain $\mathbf{e}_{r}\times\mathbf{P}_{1}=P_{0}e^{-i \omega t}(\mathbf{e}_{\varphi}\cos\theta\cos\varphi+\mathbf{e}_{\theta}\sin\varphi)$ and $\mathbf{e}_{r}\times\mathbf{P}_{2}=iP_{0}e^{-i \omega t}[\mathbf{e}_{\varphi}\cos(\varphi-\psi_{0})\cos\theta+\mathbf{e}_{\theta}\sin(\varphi-\psi_{0})]$. (a) The average power per unit solid angle is then calculated as $\frac{d\bar{S}}{d\Omega}=\frac{c}{4\pi}\mathbf{B}\cdot\mathbf{B}^{*}r^{2}=\frac{P_{0}^{2}k^{4}c}{8\pi}\left\{2-\sin^{2}\theta[\cos^{2}\varphi+\cos^{2}(\varphi-\psi_{0})]\right\}$.

raw_question_text: A waveguide is formed by two infinite parallel perfectly conducting planes separated by a distance $a$. The gap between the planes is filled with a gas whose index of refraction is $n$ (this is taken to be frequency independent). A uniform charged wire, which extends infinitely along the $y$ direction (Fig. 5.15), moves in the midplane of the gap with velocity $v>c/n$. It emits ДҢerenkov radiation. At any fixed point in the gap this reveals itself as time-varying electric and magnetic fields. How does the magnitude of the electric field vary with time at a point in the midplane of the gap?

metadata.reasoning_chain: In vacuum, the electric field at a field point created by a particle of charge $q$ moving with velocity $\mathbf{v}$ is given by $\mathbf{E}=\frac{q}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{\alpha\mathbf{R}}{s^3}$, with $\alpha=1-\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^2$ and $s=\left[\alpha R^2+\frac{1}{c^2}(\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{R})^2\right]^{1/2}$. In a medium with refractive index $n$, these modify to $\alpha=1-\left(\frac{vn}{c}\right)^2$ and $s=\left[\alpha R^2+\left(\frac{n}{c}\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{R}\right)^2\right]^{1/2}$. For a point $P$ in the midplane where $\mathbf{R}=-vt\,\mathbf{e}_x-y\,\mathbf{e}_y$, by symmetry the contributions along $y$ cancel and the net electric field is along $x$. Writing the contribution as $dE_{x}$ from a charge element and integrating up to the limit determined by the Cerenkov cone yields $E(t)\propto 1/t$, so that $E(t)=\frac{A}{t}$. A Fourier transform then gives $E(\omega)=\frac{A}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\frac{e^{i\omega t}}{t}dt=\frac{Ai}{2}$, demonstrating that each monochromatic component has the same intensity (a white spectrum) and that there is no principal frequency.

raw_question_text: In a recent classic table-top experiment, a monochromatic neutron beam ($\lambda=1.445 \AA$) was split by Bragg reflection at point $A$ of an interferometer into two beams which were recombined (after another reflection) at point $D$. One beam passes through a region of transverse magnetic field of strength $B$ for a distance $l$. Assume that the two paths from $A$ to $D$ are identical except for the region of the field. Find the explicit expressions for the dependence of the intensity at point $D$ on $\mathbf{B}$, $l$ and the neutron wavelength, with the neutron polarized either parallel or anti-parallel to the magnetic field.

metadata.reasoning_chain: This is a problem on spinor interference. Consider a neutron in the beam. There is a magnetic field $\mathbf{B}$ in the region where the SchrГ¶dinger equation for the (uncharged) neutron is $\left(-\frac{\hbar^{2}}{2m}\nabla^{2}-\mu\boldsymbol{\sigma}\cdot\mathbf{B}\right)\psi=E\psi$. Supposing $B$ to be constant and uniform, we have $\psi(t_{1})=\exp\left[-i\hat{H}(t_{1}-t_{0})/\hbar\right]\psi(t_{0})$, where $t_{0},t_{1}$ are respectively the instants when the neutron enters and leaves the magnetic field. Write $\psi(t)=\psi(\mathbf{r},t)\psi(\mathbf{s},t)$, where $\psi(\mathbf{r},t)$ and $\psi(\mathbf{s},t)$ are respectively the space and spin parts of $\psi$. Then $\psi(\mathbf{r},t_{1})=\operatorname{ex}\left[-\frac{i}{\hbar}\left(-\frac{\hbar^{2}}{2m}\nabla^{2}\right)(t_{1}-t_{0})\right]\psi(\mathbf{r},t_{0})$, which is the same as the wave function of a free particle, and $\psi(\mathbf{s},t_{1})=\exp\left[\frac{i}{\hbar}\mu\boldsymbol{\sigma}\cdot\mathbf{B}(t_{1}-t_{0})\right]\psi(s,t_{0})$. The interference arises from the action of $\mathbf{B}$ on the spin wave function. As $\psi(\mathbf{r},t)$ is the wave function of a free particle, we have $t_{1}-t_{0}=l/v=ml/\hbar k$ and $\psi(\mathbf{s},t_{1})=\exp\left[i2\pi\mu ml\lambda\boldsymbol{\sigma}\cdot\mathbf{B}/h^{2}\right]\psi(s,t_{0})$, where $k=\frac{2\pi}{\lambda}=mv/\hbar$ is the wave number of the neutron. The intensity of the interference of the two beams at $D$ is then proportional to $\left|\psi_{D}^{(1)}(\mathbf{r},t)\psi_{D}^{(1)}(\mathbf{s},t)+\psi_{D}^{(2)}(\mathbf{r},t)\psi_{D}^{(2)}(\mathbf{s},t)\right|^{2}\propto\left|\psi_{D}^{(1)}(\mathbf{s},t)+\psi_{D}^{(2)}(\mathbf{s},t)\right|^{2}=\left|\psi^{(2)}(\mathbf{s},t_{0})+\psi^{(2)}(\mathbf{s},t_{1})\right|^{2}$. As $\exp\left(i\frac{2\pi\mu ml\lambda}{h^{2}}\boldsymbol{\sigma}\cdot\mathbf{B}\right)=\cos\frac{2\pi\mu ml\lambda B}{h^{2}}+i\boldsymbol{\sigma}\cdot\frac{\mathbf{B}}{B}\sin\frac{2\pi\mu ml\lambda B}{h^{2}}$, and $\boldsymbol{\sigma}\cdot\mathbf{B}=\pm\sigma B$ depending on whether $\boldsymbol{\sigma}$ is parallel or anti-parallel to $B$, we have $\left|\psi^{(2)}(\mathbf{s},t_{0})+\psi^{(2)}(\mathbf{s},t_{1})\right|^{2}=\left|1+\exp\left(i\frac{2\pi\mu ml\lambda}{h^{2}}\boldsymbol{\sigma}\cdot\mathbf{B}\right)\right|^{2}\left|\psi(\mathbf{s},t_{0})\right|^{2}=\left(1+\cos\frac{2\pi\mu ml\lambda B}{h^{2}}\right)^{2}+\sin^{2}\frac{2\pi\mu ml\lambda B}{h^{2}}4\cos^{2}\frac{\pi\mu ml\lambda B}{h^{2}}. \text{ Therefore, the interference intensity at } D\propto\cos^{2}\left(\pi\mu ml\lambda B/h^{2}\right), \text{ where } \mu \text{ is the intrinsic magnetic moment of the neutron } (\mu<0).

metadata.reasoning_chain: Consider only one link. When an external force $f$ is exerted, the probability that the angle is $0^{\circ}$ or $180^{\circ}$ is proportional to $e^{\alpha}$ or $e^{-\alpha}$ respectively, where $\alpha=f d / k T$. The average length per link is therefore $\bar{l}=d \frac{e^{\alpha}-e^{-\alpha}}{e^{\alpha}+e^{-\alpha}}=d \tanh \alpha$. The overall length of the polymer is then $L=N \bar{l}=N d \tanh (f d / k T)$.

raw_question_text:
Consider a one-dimensional solid of length \(L=N a\) made up of \(N\) diatomic molecules, the interatomic spacing within a molecule is \(b\left(b<\frac{a}{2}\right)\). The centers of adjacent molecules are a distance \(a\) apart. We represent the potential energy as a sum of delta functions centered on each atom:
\[
V=-A \sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\left[\delta\left(x-n a+\frac{b}{2}\right)+\delta\left(x-n a-\frac{b}{2}\right)\right]
\]
with \(A\) a positive quantity and \(n=0,1,2, \ldots, N-1\). The potential is shown in Fig. 1.18. Expressing the potential as a Fourier series
\[
V=\sum_{q} V_{q} e^{i q x}
\]
find the allowed values of \(q\) and the coefficients \(V_{q}\).

metadata.reasoning_chain:
As $V(x)$ has period $a$, in the Fourier series
\[
V(x)=\sum_{q} V_{q} e^{i q x}
\]
we require
\[
e^{i q x}=e^{i q(x+a)}
\]
or
\[
q= rac{2 n \pi}{a}, \quad n=0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots
\]
which are the allowed values of $q$. Consider
\[
 egin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{a} V(x) e^{-i q^{\prime} x} d x & =\sum_{q} V_{q} \int_{0}^{a} e^{i  rac{2 \pi}{a}\left(n-n^{\prime}ight) x} d x \
& =\sum_{q} V_{q} a \delta_{q q^{\prime}}=V_{q^{\prime}} a
\end{aligned}
\]
where $q^{\prime}= rac{2 n^{\prime} \pi}{a}$ is a particular $q$. Thus the coefficients are given by
\[
V_{q}= rac{1}{a} \int_{0}^{a} V(x) e^{-i q x} d x
\]

In the neighborhood of $x=n a$,
\[
V(x)=-A\left[\delta\left(x-n a+ rac{b}{2}ight)+\delta\left(x-n a- rac{b}{2}ight)ight],
\]
so that
\[
 egin{aligned}
V_{q} & =- rac{A}{a}\left[\int_{0}^{a} \delta\left(x-n a+ rac{b}{2}ight) e^{-i q x} d x+\int_{0}^{a} \delta\left(x-n a- rac{b}{2}ight) e^{-i q x} d xight] \
& =- rac{A}{a}\left[e^{-i q\left(n a- rac{b}{2}ight)}+e^{-i q\left(n a+ rac{b}{2}ight)}ight] \
& =- rac{A}{a} e^{i q n a} \cdot 2 \cos \left( rac{b q}{2}ight) \
& =- rac{2 A}{a} \cos \left( rac{b q}{2}ight)
\end{aligned}
\]
as $e^{i q n a}=e^{i 2 n^{2} \pi}=1$.

Suppose the barriers are weak, i.e., $|t|=1$ and $|r|=0$, find a simple expression for the width of the energy gaps.

Consider an energy gap in the $k$-space with boundaries $k_{1}, k_{2}$ and midpoint at $k_{0}$ and width $2 \epsilon$. It can be seen from Fig. 1.20 and Eq. (2) that
\[
 egin{aligned}
\left|\cos \left(k_{0} a+\deltaight)ight| & =1 \
\left|\cos \left(k_{0} a+\delta+\epsilon aight)ight| & =|t|
\end{aligned}
\]
or
\[
k_{0} a+\delta=n \pi
\]
where $n$ is zero or an integer, and
\[
|\sin (n \pi+\epsilon a)|=\sqrt{1-|t|^{2}}=|r|
\]

For a weak barrier, $|r| ightarrow 0,|t| ightarrow 1,|\delta| ightarrow 0$, and as can be seen from Fig. 1.20, $\epsilon a ightarrow 0$, so that
\[
k_{0}= rac{1}{2}\left(k_{2}+k_{1}ight) \approx  rac{n \pi}{2 a}
\]

\[
|\sin (n \pi) \cos (\epsilon a)+\sin (\epsilon a) \cos (n \pi)| \approx \epsilon a=|r|
\]
i.e.,
\[
k_{2}-k_{1}= rac{|r|}{a}
\]

Hence the energy gap has width
\[
\Delta E= rac{\hbar^{2}}{2 m}\left(k_{2}^{2}-k_{1}^{2}ight)= rac{\hbar^{2}}{m} k_{0}\left(k_{2}-k_{1}ight)= rac{n \pi}{2}  rac{\hbar^{2}}{m a^{2}}|r|
\]

raw_question_text: A long thin uniformly magnetized needle of hard steel, length 12 cm, is suspended by a torsionless suspension and makes free, small-angle oscillations about an axis through the center in a horizontal plane in the earth's magnetic field. The period of oscillation is $3-5$ seconds. Estimate the density of magnetization of the steel.

metadata.reasoning_chain:
As only small-angle oscillations are involved, the earth's magnetic field can be considered uniform.
Let $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ be the magnetic moment of the steel needle. Its potential energy in the earth's magnetic field is $V=-\mu \cdot \mathbf{B}=-\mu B \cos \theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ and $\mathbf{B}$ as shown in Fig. 1.41. The equation of motion is
\[
I \ddot{\theta}=-\frac{d V}{d \theta}=-\mu B \sin \theta \approx-\mu B \theta
\]
$I$ being the moment of inertia of the needle, for small angle oscillations. The angular frequency of oscillation is given by
\[
\omega^{2}=\frac{\mu B}{I}
\]

Let $l, V, \rho, \mathbf{M}$ be respectively the length, volume, density and magnetization of the steel needle. Then
\[
I=\frac{\rho V l^{2}}{12}, \quad \boldsymbol{\mu}=V \mathbf{M}
\]
and thus
\[
\omega^{2}=\frac{12 B M}{\rho l^{2}}
\]
on
\[
M=\frac{\pi^{2} \rho l^{2}}{3 B T^{2}}
\]
where $T$ is the period of oscillation. With $l=0.12 \mathrm{~m}, T=4 \mathrm{~s}, \rho=$ $78 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{kgm}^{-3}, B \sim 5 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{Wm}^{-2}$, we find
\[
M=4.6 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{Am}^{-1}
\]

raw_question_text: A photomultiplier tube is to be used to detect light pulses each of which consists of a small but fixed number of photons. The average photoelectric efficiency is $10 \%$. That is, a photon has a $10 \%$ probability of causing the emission of a detectable photoelectron. Assume the photomultiplier gain is $10^{6}$ and that the output current as a function of time can be approximated as shown in Fig. 3.6. What is $N$, the average number of photons in each pulse?

extraction_notes: image field contains a PIL object string representation rather than a concrete file path or URL
metadata.reasoning_chain:
The total quantity of charge carried by one pulse of current is
\[
Q=\int I d t
\]
which is the area of the triangle in Fig. 3.6. Thus
\[
Q= rac{1}{2} 	imes 20 	imes 10^{-9} 	imes 80 	imes 10^{-6}=8 	imes 10^{-13} \mathrm{C}
\]
and the number of electrons carried by one pulse is
\[
n= rac{Q}{e}= rac{8 	imes 10^{-13}}{1.6 	imes 10^{-19}}=5 	imes 10^{6} .
\]

Then the number of photoelectrons emitted per light pulse is
\[
n^{\prime}=n / 10^{6}=5
\]
and hence the number of photons in one light pulse is
\[
N=n^{\prime} / 0.1=50 .
\]

raw_question_text: The simplest model for low-lying states of nuclei with $N$ and $Z$ between 20 and 28 involves only $f_{7/2}$ nucleons. Using this model predict the magnetic dipole moments of ${ }_{20}^{41} \text{Ca}_{21}$ and ${ }_{21}^{41} \text{Sc}_{20}$. Estimate crudely the electric quadrupole moments for these two cases as well.

metadata.reasoning_chain:
${ }^{41} \text{Ca}$ has a neutron and ${ }^{41} \text{Sc}$ has a proton outside closed shells in state $1 f_{7 / 2}$. As closed shells do not contribute to the nuclear magnetic moment, the latter is determined by the extra-shell nucleons. The nuclear magnetic moment is given by
$$
\boldsymbol{\mu}=g \mathbf{j} \mu_{N},
$$
where $\mathbf{j}$ is the total angular momentum, $\mu_{N}$ is the nuclear magneton. For a single nucleon in a central field, the $g$-factor is (Problem 2061)
$$
\begin{array}{ll}
g=\frac{(2 j-1) g_{l}+g_{s}}{2 j} & \text { for } j=l+\frac{1}{2}, 
g=\frac{(2 j+3) g_{l}-g_{s}}{2(j+1)} & \text { for } j=l-\frac{1}{2} .
\end{array}
$$

For neutron, $g_{l}=0, g_{s}=g_{n}=-\frac{1.91}{\frac{1}{2}}=-3.82$. As $l=3$ and $j=\frac{7}{2}=3+\frac{1}{2}$, we have for ${ }^{41} \text{Ca}$
$$
\mu\left({ }^{41} C a\right)=-\frac{3.82}{2 j} \times j \mu_{N}=-1.91 \mu_{N} .
$$

For proton, $g_{l}=1, g_{s}=g_{p}=\frac{2.79}{1 / 2}=5.58$. As $j=\frac{7}{2}=3+\frac{1}{2}$, we have for ${ }^{41} \text{Sc}$
$$
\mu\left({ }^{41} S c\right)=\frac{(7-1)+5.58}{7} \times \frac{7}{2} \mu_{N}=5.79 \mu_{N} .
$$

Note that these values are only in rough agreement with the given experimental values.

The electric quadrupole moment of ${ }^{41} \text{Sc}$, which has a single proton outside closed shells, is given by
$$
Q\left({ }^{41} S c\right)=-e^{2}\left\langle r^{2}\right\rangle \frac{2 j-1}{2(j+1)}=-\left\langle r^{2}\right\rangle \frac{2 j-1}{2(j+1)},
$$
where $\left\langle r^{2}\right\rangle$ is the mean-square distance from the center and the proton charge is taken to be one. For an order-of-magnitude estimate take $\left\langle r^{2}\right\rangle=$ $\left(1.2 \times A^{1 / 3}\right)^{2} \mathrm{fm}^{2}$. Then
$$
Q\left({ }^{41} S c\right)=-\frac{6}{9} \times\left(1.2 \times 41^{\frac{1}{3}}\right)^{2}=-1.14 \times 10^{-25} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}
$$
${ }^{41} \text{Ca}$ has a neutron outside the full shells. Its electric quadrupole moment is caused by the polarization of the neutron relative to the nucleus center and is
$$
Q\left({ }^{41} C a\right) \approx \frac{Z}{(A-1)^{2}}\left|Q\left({ }^{41} S c\right)\right|=1.43 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}
$$

raw_question_text: The simplest model for low-lying states of nuclei with $N$ and $Z$ between 20 and 28 involves only $f_{7/2}$ nucleons. What states are expected in ${}_{20}^{42}\text{Ca}$ according to an application of this model? Calculate the magnetic dipole and electric quadrupole moments for these states. Sketch the complete decay sequence expected experimentally for the highest spin state.

extraction_notes: images field contains a PIL object string representation rather than a file path or URL

metadata.reasoning_chain:
As shown in Fig. 2.15 the ground state of ${ }^{42} \text{Ca}$ nucleus is $0^{+}$. The two last neutrons, which are in $f_{7 / 2}$ can be coupled to form levels of $J=$ $7,6,5 \ldots, 0$ and positive parity. Taking into account the antisymmetry for identical particles, the possible levels are those with $J=6,4,2,0$. (We require $L+S=$ even, see Problem 2054. As $S=0, J=$ even.)
The magnetic dipole moment $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ of a two-nucleon system is given by
$$
\boldsymbol{\mu}=g \mathbf{J} \mu_{N}=\left(g_{1} \mathbf{j}_{1}+g_{2} \mathbf{j}_{2}\right) \mu_{N}
$$
with $\mathbf{J}=\mathbf{j}_{1}+\mathbf{j}_{2}$. As
$$
\begin{aligned}
g \mathbf{J}^{2} & =g_{1} \mathbf{j}_{1} \cdot \mathbf{J}+g_{2} \mathbf{j}_{2} \cdot \mathbf{J}, 
\mathbf{j}_{1} \cdot \mathbf{J} & =\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathbf{J}^{2}+\mathbf{j}_{1}^{2}-\mathbf{j}_{2}^{2}\right), 
\mathbf{j}_{2} \cdot \mathbf{J} & =\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathbf{J}^{2}+\mathbf{j}_{2}^{2}-\mathbf{j}_{1}^{2}\right),
\end{aligned}
$$
we have
$$
g \mathbf{J}^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left(g_{1}+g_{2}\right) \mathbf{J}^{2}+\frac{1}{2}\left(g_{1}-g_{2}\right)\left(\mathbf{j}_{1}^{2}-\mathbf{j}_{2}^{2}\right) .
$$
or
$$
g=\frac{1}{2}\left(g_{1}+g_{2}\right)+\frac{1}{2}\left(g_{1}-g_{2}\right) \frac{j_{1}\left(j_{1}+1\right)-j_{2}\left(j_{2}+1\right)}{J(J+1)} .
$$

For ${ }^{42} \text{Ca}$, the two nucleons outside full shells each has $j=7 / 2$. As
$$
g_{1}=g_{2}=\frac{-3.82}{j_{1}}, \quad j_{1}=\frac{7}{2},
$$
we have $\mu\left({ }^{42} C a\right)=g_{1} J \mu_{N}=-1.09 J \mu_{N}$ with $J=0,2,4,6$.
The ground-state quadrupole moment of ${ }^{42} \text{Ca}$ is $Q=0$. One can get the excited state quadrupole moment using the reduced transition rate for $\gamma$-transition
$$
B\left(E 2,2^{+} \rightarrow 0^{+}\right)=\frac{e^{2} Q_{0}^{2}}{16 \pi}
$$
where $Q_{0}$ is the intrinsic electric quadrupole moment. The first excited state $2^{+}$of ${ }^{42} \text{Ca}$ has excitation energy 1.524 MeV and
$$
B\left(E 2: 2^{+} \rightarrow 0^{+}\right)=81.5 e^{2} \mathrm{fm}^{4},
$$
or
$$
Q_{0}=\sqrt{16 \pi \times 81.5}=64 \mathrm{fm}^{2} .
$$

For other states the quadrupole moments are given by
$$
Q=\frac{K^{2}-J(J+1)}{(J+1)(2 J+3)} Q_{0}=-\frac{J(J+1) Q_{0}}{(J+1)(2 J+3)}=\frac{-J}{2 J+3} Q_{0}
$$
as $K=0$. Thus $Q=18.3 \mathrm{fm}^{2}$ for $J=2,23.3 \mathrm{fm}^{2}$ for $J=4$, and $25.6 \mathrm{fm}^{2}$ for $J=6$.

metadata.reasoning_chain: The neutron magic numbers between $^{40}\text{Ca}$ and $^{208}\text{Pb}$ are 28,50 and 82. At $\text{N}=82$, the separation energies are about 12 MeV. However, for heavy nuclei, there are more neutrons than protons, so $A<2N$. On account of this, for the nuclei of magic numbers 50 and 82, the separation energies are somewhat less than those given above. At the magic number 28 the separation energy is highest, and so this is the best neutron magic number.

Consider the following energy level structure (Fig. 2.30): The ground states form an isotriplet as do the excited states (all states have a spin-parity of $0^{+}$). The ground state of $_{21}^{42} \text{Sc}$ can $\beta$-decay to the ground state of $_{20}^{42} \text{Ca}$ with a kinetic end-point energy of 5.4 MeV (transition II in Fig. 2.30). Consider isospin mixing due to electromagnetic interactions. In general 
 H_{E M}=H_{0}+H_{1}+H_{2}, 
 where the subscripts refer to the isospin tensor rank of each term. Write the branching ratio $\frac{\text{О“}_{I}}{\text{О“}_{II}}$ in terms of the reduced matrix elements of each part of $H_{E M}$ which mixes the states.

metadata.reasoning_chain:
We make use of the perturbation theory. Let the ground and excited states of $^{42}\text{Ca}$ be $|1\rangle$ and $|2\rangle$ respectively. Because of the effect of $H_{EM}$, the states become mixed. Let the mixed states be $|1\rangle^{\prime}$ and $|2\rangle^{\prime}$, noting that the mixing due to $H_{EM}$ is very small. We have
$$\begin{aligned}
H^{0}|1\rangle & =E_{1}|1\rangle, 
H^{0}|2\rangle & =E_{2}|2\rangle,
\end{aligned}$$
where $E_{1}$ and $E_{2}$ are the energies of the two states $(E_{1} \approx E_{0}, E_{2}-E_{1}=1.8 \text{ MeV})$.

Consider
$$ H=H^{0}+H_{EM}, $$
where $H_{EM}=H_{0}+H_{1}+H_{2}$. As the index refers to isospin tensor rank, we write $H_{0}, H_{1}, H_{2}$ as $P_{0,0}, P_{1,0}, P_{2,0}$ and define
$$ \langle J_{1} m_{1} | P_{\mu 
u} | J_{2} m_{2} \rangle = C_{\mu 
u J_{2} m_{2}}^{J_{1} m_{1}} \langle J_{1} | P_{\mu 
u} | J_{2} \rangle. $$
Then
$$\begin{aligned}
H_{EM}= & P_{0,0}+P_{1,0}+P_{2,0} 
\langle 1| H_{EM}|2\rangle= & \langle \alpha, 1,-1| (P_{0,0}+P_{1,0}+P_{2,0}) |\alpha^{\prime}, 1,-1\rangle 
= & \left( \langle \alpha, 1| P_{0} |\alpha^{\prime}, 1\rangle - \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} \langle \alpha, 1| P_{1} |\alpha^{\prime}, 1\rangle \right) 
& + \sqrt{\frac{1}{10}} \langle \alpha, 1| P_{2} |\alpha^{\prime}, 1\rangle 
\langle 1| H_{EM}|1\rangle & =\langle 2| H_{EM}|2\rangle=\langle \alpha, 1,-1| (P_{0,0}+P_{1,0}+P_{2,0}) |\alpha, 1,-1\rangle 
= & \langle \alpha, 1| P_{0} |\alpha, 1\rangle - \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} \langle \alpha, 1| P_{1} |\alpha, 1\rangle + \sqrt{\frac{1}{10}} \langle \alpha, 1| P_{2} |\alpha, 1\rangle.\end{aligned}$$
In the above equations, $\alpha$ and $\alpha^{\prime}$ denote the quantum numbers of $|1\rangle$ and $|2\rangle$ other than the isospin, and $\langle \alpha, 1| P |\alpha, 1\rangle$ denote the relevant part of the reduced matrix element. Thus
$$
\frac{\Gamma_{I}}{\Gamma_{II}}=\frac{E_{1}^{5} \langle M_{1} \rangle^{2}}{E_{2}^{5} \langle M_{2} \rangle^{2}}=\frac{(5.4-1.8-\langle 2| H_{EM}|2\rangle)^{5}}{(5.4-\langle 1| H_{EM}|1\rangle)^{5}} \frac{\langle 1| H_{EM}|2\rangle^{2}}{(E_{2}-E_{1})^{2}}.
$$

If energy level corrections can be ignored, then $\langle 1| H_{EM}|1\rangle \ll E_{1}, E_{2}$, and
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Gamma_{I}}{\Gamma_{II}} & \approx \frac{E_{10}^{5}}{E_{20}^{5} (E_{2}-E_{1})^{2}} \langle \langle 1| H_{EM} |2\rangle \rangle 
& =\frac{(5.4-1.8)^{5}}{5.4^{5} \times 1.8^{2}} \left( \langle 1| P_{0} |2\rangle - \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} \langle 1| P_{1} |2\rangle + \sqrt{\frac{1}{10}} \langle 1| P_{2} |2\rangle \right)^{2}.
\end{aligned}$$
If we ignore the contribution of $H_{2}$ and assume $\langle 1| P_{0} |2\rangle=0$, then the isoscalar $H$ does not mix the two isospin states and we have
$$
\frac{\Gamma_{I}}{\Gamma_{II}}=\frac{E_{10}^{5}}{E_{20}^{5} (E_{2}-E_{1})^{2}}|\langle \alpha, 1| P_{1} |\alpha^{\prime}, 1\rangle|.
$$

raw_question_text: Inelastic neutrino scattering in the quark model. Consider the scattering of neutrinos on free, massless quarks. We will simplify things and discuss only strangeness-conserving reactions, i.e. transitions only between the $u$ and $d$ quarks. The experimental value is $\sigma^{\bar{u} N} / \sigma^{u N} = 0.37 \pm 0.02$. What does this value tell you about the quark/antiquark structure of the nucleon?

extraction_notes: answer field is numeric only; reasoning indicates it is the antiquark-to-quark ratio $\alpha$
image entries are serialized PIL object strings rather than actual file paths or URLs
metadata.reasoning_chain:
The experimental value $\sigma\left(\bar{u} N\right) / \sigma\left(u N\right)=0.37 \pm 0.02$ is approximately the same as the theoretical value $1 / 3$. This means that nucleons consist mainly of quarks, any antiquarks present would be very small in proportion. Let the ratio of antiquark to quark in a nucleon be $\alpha$, then
$\frac{\sigma\left(\bar{u} N\right)}{\sigma\left(u N\right)}=\frac{3 \sigma\left(\bar{u} u\right)+3 \alpha \sigma\left(\bar{u} \bar{d}\right)}{3 \sigma\left(u d\right)+3 \alpha \sigma\left(u \bar{u}\right)}=\frac{3 \times \frac{1}{3}+3 \alpha \times 1}{3 \times 1+3 \alpha \times \frac{1}{3}}=\frac{1+3 \alpha}{3+\alpha}=0.37$
giving
$\frac{1+3 \alpha}{8}=\frac{0.37}{2.63}$
or
$\alpha=4 \times 10^{-2}$

raw_question_text:
The "charmed" particles observed in $e^{+} e^{-}$storage rings have not yet been seen in hadron-hadron interactions. One possible means for detecting such particles is the observation of muons resulting from their leptonic decays. For example, consider a charmed particle $c$ with decay mode 
$$ c \rightarrow \mu 
u $$ Unfortunately, the experimental situation is complicated by the presence of muons from $\pi$ decays. Consider an experiment at Fermilab in which 400 GeV protons strike a thick iron target (beam dump) as depicted in Fig. 4.13. Some of the muons entering the detector will be from $\pi$ decays and some from $c$ decays (ignore other processes). Calculate the ratio of muons from $c$ decays to those from $\pi$ decays under the following assumptions: the pions that have suffered interaction in the dump completely disappear from the beam, the energy spectra of both $\pi$ and $c$ are flat from minimum up to the maximum possible energy, the mass of the $c$ is $2 \mathrm{GeV} / c^{2}$ and its lifetime is $\ll 10^{-10} \mathrm{sec}$, one can ignore muon energy loss in the iron, one can ignore any complications due to the geometry of the muon detector, the $p-p$ inelastic cross section is 30 mb , and the mean charged pion multiplicity in inelastic interactions is 8 . Be specific. State any additional assumptions. Give a numerical value for the ratio at $E_{\mu}=100 \mathrm{GeV}$ assuming the total production cross section for $c$ to be $10 \mu b$ per Fe nucleus and that it decays to $\mu 
u 10 \%$ of the time.

metadata.reasoning_chain:
In addition to the assumptions listed in the question, we also assume the charge independence of nucleon interactions so that $\sigma_{p p}=\sigma_{p n}$ and the mean charged-pion multiplicities are the same for $p p$ and $p n$ collisions.

For ${ }^{56} \mathrm{Fe}$, the number densities of protons and neutrons are the same, being
$$
N_{p}=N_{n}=\frac{28}{56} \times 7.8 \times 6.02 \times 10^{23}=2.35 \times 10^{24} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}
$$

Denote the flux of protons in the incident beam by $\phi(x)$, where $x$ is the target thickness from the surface of incidence. As
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d \phi}{d x} & =-\left(\sigma_{p p} N_{p}+\sigma_{p n} N_{n}\right) \phi=-2 \sigma_{p p} N_{p} \phi 
\phi & =\phi_{0} e^{-2 \sigma_{p p} N_{p} x}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the target is sufficiently thick, say $x=10 \mathrm{~m}=10^{3} \mathrm{~cm}$, then
$$
\phi=\phi_{0} \exp \left(-2 \times 30 \times 10^{-27} \times 2.35 \times 10^{24} \times 10^{3}\right)=5.8 \times 10^{-62} \phi_{0},
$$
at the exit surface, showing that the beam of protons is completely dissipated in the target. This will be assumed in the following.

Consider first the $c$ quarks produced in $p-\mathrm{Fe}$ interactions in the target. From the given data $\sigma_{p \mathrm{Fe}}(c)=10 \mu b, \sigma_{p p}=30 \mathrm{mb}$, we find the number of $c$ quarks so produced as
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{c} & =\int N_{\mathrm{Fe}} \sigma(c) d \phi \approx N_{\mathrm{Fe}} \sigma(c) \phi_{0} \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-2 \sigma_{p p} N_{p} x} d x 
& =\frac{N_{\mathrm{Fe}}}{2 N_{p p}} \frac{\sigma(c)}{\sigma_{p p}} \phi_{0}=\frac{1}{56} \times \frac{10^{-5}}{30 \times 10^{-3}} \phi_{0}=5.95 \times 10^{-6} \phi_{0}
\end{aligned}
$$

As the $c$ quarks have lifetime $\ll 10^{-10}$ s, all those produced in $p-\mathrm{Fe}$ interactions will decay in the target, giving rise to muons $10 \%$ of the times. Thus
$$
N_{\mu c}=0.1 N_{c}=5.95 \times 10^{-7} \phi_{0} .
$$

Next consider the muons arising from the decay of charged pions produced in $p$-nucleon interactions. After emission the pions may interact
with the nucleons of the target and disappear from the beam, as assumed, or decay in flight giving rise to muons. For the former case we assume $\sigma_{\pi p}=\sigma_{\pi n} \approx \frac{2}{3} \sigma_{p p}=20 \mathrm{mb}$ at high energies. For the latter case the lifetime of the charged pions in the laboratory is $\gamma_{\pi} / \lambda$, where $\lambda$ is the decay constant and $\gamma_{\pi}=\left(1-\beta_{\pi}^{2}\right)^{-\frac{1}{2}}, \beta_{\pi} c$ being the mean velocity of the pions. Then the change of $N_{\pi}$ per unit interval of $x$ is
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d N_{\pi}}{d x} & =8\left(\sigma_{p p} N_{p}+\sigma_{p n} N_{n}\right) \phi(x)-\left(\frac{\lambda}{\gamma_{\pi} \beta_{\pi} c}+\sigma_{\pi p} N_{p}+\sigma_{\pi n} N_{n}\right) N_{\pi} 
& =16 \sigma_{p p} N_{p} \varphi_{0} e^{-2 \sigma_{p p} N_{p} x}-\left(\frac{\lambda}{\gamma_{\pi} \beta_{\pi} c}+2 \sigma_{\pi p} N_{p}\right) N_{\pi} 
& =8 B \phi_{0} e^{-B x}-B^{\prime} N_{\pi}
\end{aligned}
$$
where $B=2 \sigma_{p p} N_{p}, B^{\prime}=2 \sigma_{\pi p} N_{p}+\lambda^{\prime}, \lambda^{\prime}=\frac{\lambda}{\gamma_{\pi} \beta_{\pi c}}$. The solution of the differential equation is
$$
N_{\pi}=\frac{8 B}{B^{\prime}-B}\left(e^{-B x}-e^{-B^{\prime} x}\right) \phi_{0} .
$$

Hence the number of charged pions which decay in the target per unit interval of $x$ is
$$
\frac{d N_{\pi}(\lambda)}{d x}=\frac{\lambda}{\gamma_{\pi} \beta_{\pi} c} N_{\pi}(\lambda)=\frac{8 B \lambda^{\prime}}{B^{\prime}-B}\left(e^{-B x}-e^{-B^{\prime} x}\right) \phi_{0} .
$$

Integration from $x=0$ to $x=\infty$ gives
$$
N_{\pi}(\lambda)=\frac{8 B \lambda^{\prime}}{B^{\prime}-B}\left(\frac{1}{B}-\frac{1}{B^{\prime}}\right) \phi_{0}=\frac{8 \lambda^{\prime} \phi_{0}}{B^{\prime}} .
$$

The branching ratio for $\pi \rightarrow \mu 
u \approx 100 \%$, so that $N_{\mu \pi} \approx N_{\pi}(\lambda)$. This means that the energy spectrum of muons is also flat (though in actual fact high-energy muons are more likely than low-energy ones), making the comparison with $N_{\mu c}$ much simpler.

Take for example $E_{\mu} \sim 100 \mathrm{GeV}$. Then $E_{\pi} \gtrsim 100 \mathrm{GeV}, \beta_{\pi} \approx 1, \gamma_{\pi} \gtrsim$ 714 , and so
$$
\lambda^{\prime}=\frac{\lambda}{\gamma_{\pi} \beta_{\pi} c}=\frac{1}{2.6 \times 10^{-8} \times 714 \times 3 \times 10^{10}}=1.8 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}
$$
As
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\pi p} N_{p} & =20 \times 10^{-27} \times 2.34 \times 10^{24}=4.7 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1} \gg \lambda^{\prime} 
N_{\pi}(\lambda) & \simeq \frac{8 \lambda^{\prime} \phi_{0}}{2 \sigma_{\pi p} N_{p}}=\frac{8 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-6} \phi_{0}}{2 \times 4.7 \times 10^{-2}}=1.5 \times 10^{-4} \phi_{0}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence
$$
\frac{N_{\mu c}}{N_{\mu \pi}}=\frac{5.95 \times 10^{-7}}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}=4 \times 10^{-3}
$$

raw_question_text:
A point source $S$ located at the origin of a coordinate system emits a spherical sinusoidal wave in which the electric field $E_{1}$ is given by $E_{1}=$ $A\left( rac{D}{r}ight) \cos \left(\omega t- rac{2 \pi r}{\lambda}ight)$, where $r$ is the distance from $S$. In addition, there is a plane wave propagating along the $x$-axis. This wave is given by
\[
E_{2}=A \cos \left(\omega t- rac{2 \pi x}{\lambda}ight) .
\]
(Note that we treat both $E_{1}$ and $E_{2}$ as scalar waves in this problem.) Both waves are incident on a flat screen perpendicular to the $x$-axis and at a distance $D$ from the origin, as shown in the figure (Fig. 2.1). Compute the resultant intensity $I$ at the screen as a function of the distance $y$ from the $x$-axis for values of $y$ small compared with $D$. Express $I$ in terms of $y, D, \lambda$ and the intensity $I_{0}$ at $y=0$.

extraction_notes: image field contains a PIL image placeholder string rather than a file path or URL
metadata.reasoning_chain:
The electric field $E$ at a point, distance $r$ from the origin, on the screen is given by
$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= E_{1} + E_{2} = A\left[\frac{D}{r} \cos\left(\omega t - \frac{2\pi r}{\lambda}\right) + \cos\left(\omega t - \frac{2\pi x}{\lambda}\right)\right] \\
&\approx A\left[\cos\left(\omega t - \frac{2\pi r}{\lambda}\right) + \cos\left(\omega t - \frac{2\pi x}{\lambda}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$
where we have used the approximation $r \approx D$.

For $y \ll D$, we have
$$
r = \left(D^{2} + y^{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}} \approx D\left(1 + \frac{y^{2}}{2D^{2}}\right)
$$
and thus
$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= A\left[\cos\left(\omega t - \frac{2\pi D\left(1 + \frac{y^{2}}{2D^{2}}\right)}{\lambda}\right) + \cos\left(\omega t - \frac{2\pi D}{\lambda}\right)\right] \\
&= 2A \cos\left(\frac{\pi y^{2}}{D\lambda}\right) \cdot \cos\left(\omega t - \frac{2\pi D\left(1 + \frac{y^{2}}{4D^{2}}\right)}{\lambda}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence
$$
I \propto E^{2} \propto \cos^{2}\left(\frac{\pi y^{2}}{D\lambda}\right)
$$

Let $I = I_{0}$ at $y = 0$; we then have
$$
I = I_{0} \cos^{2}\left(\frac{\pi y^{2}}{D\lambda}\right)
$$

As the distance from the centre increases, the fringes become closer. This is similar to Fresnel's zone plate.

raw_question_text: A two-slit diffraction pattern is produced by the arrangement shown in Fig. 2.9. A discharge tube produces light of wavelength $\lambda$ which passes through a small slit $S$ immediately in front of the tube. The centers of the two slits of width $w$ are at distance $D$ apart. Find the condition that a maximum intensity be observed at the screen a distance $l$ from the central plane.

extraction_notes: answer field uses $I$, while reasoning states $l = \\frac{n\\lambda L}{D}$
metadata.reasoning_chain:
The intensity distribution on the screen produced by the elemental slit of width $dy$ of the slit sources is given by
$$
dI = 2I_{0}\left(\frac{\sin \beta}{\beta}\right)^{2}(1 + \cos \delta) dy
$$
where $\beta = \frac{\pi w l}{\lambda L} = \frac{\pi w \sin \theta}{\lambda}$ with $\sin \theta = \frac{l}{L}$, $\delta = \delta_{1} + \delta_{2}$ with
$$
\delta_{1} = \frac{2\pi D \sin \theta}{\lambda}, \quad \delta_{2} = \frac{2\pi D y}{\lambda x}
$$
and $I_{0}$ is a constant. Integration yields the intensity distribution produced by the slit source:
$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= 2I_{0}\left(\frac{\sin \beta}{\beta}\right)^{2} \int_{-\frac{s}{2}}^{\frac{s}{2}}(1 + \cos \delta) dy \\
&= 2I_{0} S\left(\frac{\sin \beta}{\beta}\right)^{2}\left\{1 + \frac{\sin\left(\frac{\pi D S}{\lambda x}\right)}{\left(\frac{\pi D S}{\lambda x}\right)} \cos\left(\frac{2\pi D}{\lambda} \sin \theta\right)\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

If $\frac{2\pi D \sin \theta}{\lambda} = \frac{2\pi D l}{\lambda L} = 2n\pi$, i.e., $l = \frac{n\lambda L}{D}$, where $n$ is an integer, maximum intensity is observed on the screen at distance $l$ from the central plane.

raw_question_text: Light of wavelength $\lambda$ and intensity $I_{0}$ is incident perpendicularly onto an opaque circular disk of radius $R$. In each case, find the intensity of the light at a distance $L$ behind the obstacle, on the path passing through the center of the circle. Take $L \gg R$.

metadata.reasoning_chain:
For diffraction by the opaque circular disk, the field $A^{\prime}$ at $P$ is given by Babinet's principle
$$
A + A^{\prime} = A_{\infty} = A_{0} e^{i k L}
$$
where $A$ is the field at $P$ due to diffraction by the circular hole above, $A_{\infty}$ is the field due to diffraction by a hole of infinite size. Thus
$$
A^{\prime} = A_{0} e^{i k L} - A = A_{0} \exp\left[i k\left(L + \frac{R^{2}}{2 L}\right)\right]
$$
giving
$$
I^{\prime} \sim \left|A^{\prime}\right|^{2} = I_{0}
$$
i.e., the intensity is nearly the same as that without any diffracting screen provided the opaque obstacle is small.

raw_question_text:
A plane wave with wavenumber $k$ is incident on a slit of width $a$. The slit is covered by a transparent wedge whose thickness is proportional to the distance from the top of the slit $(t = \gamma x)$, see Fig.\ 2.36. The index of refraction of the transparent wedge is $n$. The intensity of light at an angle $\theta$ is given by
$$
I \propto \frac{\sin^2(\beta a)}{(\beta a)^2}
$$

Derive an expression for $\beta$ in terms of $k$, $n$, $\gamma$ and $\theta$.

extraction_notes: image field contains a PIL object string representation rather than a file path or URL
raw_record.answer differs from the formula stated in reasoning
metadata.reasoning_chain:
For a wedge with thickness $t = \gamma x$, the apex angle (in radians) is $\gamma$ to first order approximation. The optical path difference introduced by the wedge causes an angular deviation of transmitted light given by:
$$
\delta = (n - 1)\gamma
$$

The single-slit diffraction intensity distribution follows the standard Fraunhofer pattern:
$$
I(\theta) = I_0 \left(\frac{\sin \beta}{\beta}\right)^2 
$$
where the diffraction parameter is:
$$
\beta \equiv \frac{\pi a}{\lambda} \sin\theta = \frac{1}{2} a k \sin\theta
$$
with $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ being the wavenumber and $\theta$ the observation angle relative to the optical axis.

When the wedge prism covers the slit, the angular deviation modifies the diffraction pattern through coordinate transformation $\theta \to \theta - \delta$:
$$
I(\theta) = I_0 \left(
\frac{\sin\left[\frac{\pi a}{\lambda}\sin(\theta - \delta)\right]}
{\frac{\pi a}{\lambda}\sin(\theta - \delta)}
\right)^2
= I_0 \left(
\frac{\sin\left\{\frac{1}{2} a k \sin[\theta - (n-1)\gamma]\right\}}
{\frac{1}{2} a k \sin[\theta - (n-1)\gamma]}
\right)^2
$$

Key observations:
\begin{itemize}
\item The characteristic $\sinc^2$ intensity profile is preserved
\item The entire diffraction pattern shifts by $\delta = (n-1)\gamma$
\item The angular shift is wavelength-independent for small $\gamma$
\item Central maximum occurs when $\theta = (n-1)\gamma$
\end{itemize}

This results in the modified diffraction parameter:
$$
\beta = \frac{\pi a}{\lambda} \sin[\theta - (n-1)\gamma] = \frac{1}{2} a k \sin[\theta - (n-1)\gamma]
$$

raw_question_text: A beam of light, with $\lambda=5000 \AA$, traveling in the $z$-direction is polarised at $45^{\circ}$ to the $x$-direction. It passes through a Kerr cell, i.e., a substance such that $n_{x}-n_{y}=K E^{2}$, where $n_{x}$ and $n_{y}$ are the refractive indices for light polarized in the $x$ and $y$ directions. $E$ is the strength of an externally applied electric field in the $x$-direction. The cell has length 1 cm and $K=2.5 \times 10^{-6}$ (meter) $^{2} /(\text { volts })^{2}$. If the light, after passing through the Kerr cell, passes through a polarization analyzer whose plane of polarisation is perpendicular to that of the original beam, calculate the smallest value of $E$ which gives maximum transmission. force_requires_image set from record structure including vision_relevance="essential" and image caption, not from explicit wording in the question
metadata.reasoning_chain:
The Kerr cell induces birefringence. As the direction of the electric field is $45^\circ$ to the direction of polarization of the light, the amplitudes of the components polarized parallel to the $x$ and $y$ directions are the same. When the phase difference $\delta$ between the components is
$$
\delta = \frac{2\pi l(n_{x}-n_{y})}{\lambda} = \frac{2\pi l K E^{2}}{\lambda} = (2j+1)\pi, \quad j=0 \text{ or integer},
$$
the Kerr cell acts as a half-wave plate and rotates the plane of polarization by $90^\circ$ so that it coincides with the transmission axis of the analyzer and gives maximum transmission. Putting $j=0$ yields the smallest $E$, $E_{\min}$, which is given by
$$
E_{\min}^{2} = \frac{\lambda}{2 K l} = 10~\mathrm{V}^{2}/\mathrm{m}^{2} \text{ for } l=1~\mathrm{cm}.
$$

metadata.reasoning_chain:
As 
$$ 
T \propto \alpha^{1/4} 
$$ 
when $\alpha^{\prime} = \alpha/2$, we have 
$$ 
T^{\prime} = (1/2)^{1/4}T = 286\,\mathrm{K} 
$$ 
Thus if the absorptance dropped by $1/2$, the maximum temperature would be 286 K.

subject: зү©зҗҶ
raw_question_text: An insulating spherical shell of radius $R$ carries surface charge density $\sigma(\theta)=\sigma_{o} \cos \theta$, where $\sigma_{o}$ is a constant. The shell is sent spinning around the $x$-axis $(\vec{\omega}=\omega \hat{x})$. Calculate the power radiated.

extraction_notes: images field contains a PIL image object string rather than a file path or URL
question text does not explicitly reference a figure, but raw_record.vision_relevance is marked as essential
metadata.reasoning_chain:
Solution 5.2. To find the power radiated, we can think of this as a dipole which is given by
\[
\vec{p}=p_{o}[\cos (\omega t) \hat{z}-\sin (\omega t) \hat{y}]
\]
and the power radiated is given by
\[
P=\frac{\mu_{o} \ddot{o}^{2}}{6 \pi c}
\]

Noting
\[
\ddot{\vec{p}}=-\omega^{2} \vec{p}
\]
it follows that $\ddot{p}^{2}=\omega^{4} p_{o}^{2}$. We must now find $p_{o}$ from the charge distribution using
\[
\vec{p}_{o}=\int \vec{r}^{\prime} \rho\left(\vec{r}^{\prime}\right) \mathrm{d} \tau^{\prime}=\int \vec{r}^{\prime} \sigma_{o} \cos \theta \mathrm{~d} a^{\prime}
\]

Since we are in spherical coordinates
\[
\vec{r}=R[\sin \theta(\cos \phi \hat{x}+\sin \phi \hat{y})+\cos \theta \hat{z}]
\]
and
\[
\mathrm{d} a=R^{2} \sin \theta \mathrm{~d} \phi \mathrm{~d} \theta
\]

So
\[
\begin{aligned}
\vec{p}_{o}= & \sigma_{o} \int_{0}^{\pi} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} R^{3} \sin \theta \cos \theta[\sin \theta(\cos \phi \hat{x}+\sin \phi \hat{y})+\cos \theta \hat{z}] \mathrm{d} \phi \mathrm{~d} \theta \\
= & \sigma_{o} R^{3}\left[\hat{x} \int_{0}^{\pi} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} \sin ^{2} \theta \cos \theta \cos \phi \mathrm{~d} \phi \mathrm{~d} \theta+\hat{y} \int_{0}^{\pi} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} \sin ^{2} \theta \cos \theta \sin \phi \mathrm{~d} \phi \mathrm{~d} \theta\right. \\
& \left.+\hat{z} \int_{0}^{\pi} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} \sin \theta \cos ^{2} \theta \mathrm{~d} \phi \mathrm{~d} \theta\right]
\end{aligned}
\]
The $\hat{x}$ and $\hat{y}$ integrals are both zero and
\[
\hat{z} \int_{0}^{\pi} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} \sin \theta \cos ^{2} \theta \mathrm{~d} \phi \mathrm{~d} \theta=\frac{4 \pi}{3} \hat{z}
\]

Therefore,
\[
\vec{p}_{o}=\sigma_{o} \frac{4 \pi}{3} R^{3} \hat{z}
\]
and
\[
\ddot{p}^{2}=\omega^{4} p_{o}^{2}=\omega^{4} \sigma_{o}^{2} \frac{16 \pi^{2}}{9} R^{6}
\]

The power radiated is then given by
\[
P=\frac{\mu_{o} \ddot{p}^{2}}{6 \pi c}=\frac{\mu_{o}}{6 \pi c} \omega^{4} \sigma_{o}^{2} \frac{16 \pi^{2}}{9} R^{6}=\frac{8 \pi \mu_{o} \sigma_{o}^{2} \omega^{4} R^{6}}{27 c}
\]

subject: зү©зҗҶ
raw_question_text: Consider an infinite sheet in the $x y$-plane carrying surface current $\\vec{K}(t)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} 0, & t \leqslant 0 \\ K \\hat{x}, & t>0 \end{array}\right.$. Find the generated magnetic field.

metadata.reasoning_chain:
Solution 4.7. Since $\rho=0$, we have $V=0$. At a point P in the figure below, we have vector potential
\[
\vec{A}=\frac{\mu_{o}}{4 \pi} \int \frac{K \hat{x}}{r} \mathrm{~d} a=\frac{\mu_{o} K \hat{x}}{4 \pi} \int \frac{1}{r} \mathrm{~d} a
\]

Note that for a time $t<\frac{z}{c}$, the news that the current has been turned on has not arrived yet. So, for $t \geqslant \frac{z}{c}$, the area contained in
\[
r^{2} \leqslant(c t)^{2}-z^{2} \Rightarrow r \leqslant \sqrt{(c t)^{2}-z^{2}}
\]
is the only part of the sheet that contributes to the fields. We can rewrite our potential as
\[
\vec{A}=\frac{\mu_{o} K \hat{x}}{4 \pi} \int_{0}^{\sqrt{(c t)^{2}-z^{2}}} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} \frac{r}{r} \mathrm{~d} \phi \mathrm{~d} r
\]
where $r=\sqrt{z^{2}+r^{2}}$. Therefore,
\[
\vec{A}=\frac{\mu_{o} K \hat{x}}{4 \pi} 2 \pi \int_{0}^{\sqrt{(c t)^{2}-z^{2}}} \frac{r}{\sqrt{z^{2}+r^{2}}} \mathrm{~d} r=\frac{\mu_{o} K \hat{x}}{2}(c t-z)
\]

Now our electric field is given by
\[
\vec{E}=-\frac{\partial \vec{A}}{\partial t}=-\frac{\mu_{o} K \hat{x}}{2} \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(c t-z)=-\frac{\mu_{o} c K \hat{x}}{2}
\]
and our magnetic field is given by
\[
\begin{aligned}
\vec{B} & =\nabla \times \vec{A}=\frac{\mu_{o} K}{2}[\nabla \times[\hat{x}(c t-z)]] \\
& =\frac{\mu_{o} K}{2} \frac{\partial}{\partial z}(c t-z) \hat{y}=-\frac{\mu_{o} K}{2} \hat{y}
\end{aligned}
\]
which is what we would expect in the static case.

metadata.reasoning_chain:
гҖҗи§ЈгҖ‘з”ұзғӯеҠӣеӯҰз¬¬дёҖе®ҡеҫӢпјҢеҜ№$CA$дёӯд»»дёҖе…ғиҝҮзЁӢпјҢеӣ ж°Ұж°”дёә1 molпјҢжңү
$$
\mathrm{d}Q = \mathrm{d}U + p \mathrm{d}V = C_V \mathrm{d}T + p \mathrm{d}V = \frac{3}{2} R \mathrm{d}T + p \mathrm{d}V
$$

зҠ¶жҖҒж–№зЁӢдёә
$$
pV = RT
$$

$CA$иҝҮзЁӢж–№зЁӢдёә
$$
p = \alpha - \beta V
$$

еӣ $p_C = 2p_1$, $V_C = V_1$, $p_A = p_1$, $V_A = 2V_1$пјҢеҫ—$\alpha = 3p_1$, $\beta = \frac{p_1}{V_1}$пјҢж•…иҝҮзЁӢж–№зЁӢдёә
$$
p = 3p_1 - \frac{p_1}{V_1} V
$$

ејҸ(1)~ејҸ(3)иҒ”з«ӢпјҢж¶ҲеҺ»$p$е’Ң$T$пјҢеҫ—
$$
\mathrm{d}Q = \frac{p_1}{2V_1} (15V_1 - 8V) \mathrm{d}V
$$

еңЁиҪ¬жҠҳзӮ№пјҲ$D$зӮ№пјү
$$
\mathrm{d}Q = 0
$$

ж•…$D$зӮ№зҡ„дҪ“з§Ҝдёә
$$
V_D = \frac{15}{8} V_1
$$

еңЁ$CD$иҝҮзЁӢдёӯпјҢ$V < V_D$, $\mathrm{d}Q > 0$пјҢеҗёзғӯпјӣеңЁ$DA$иҝҮзЁӢдёӯпјҢ$V > V_D$, $\mathrm{d}Q < 0$пјҢж”ҫзғӯгҖӮжҠҠејҸ(4)д»Је…ҘејҸ(3)е’ҢејҸ(2)пјҢеҫ—
$$
p_D = \frac{9}{8} p_1, \quad RT_D = \frac{9}{8} p_1 \cdot \frac{15}{8} V_1 = \frac{135}{64} p_1 V_1
$$

еҫӘзҺҜиҝҮзЁӢ$ABCA$зҡ„ж•ҲзҺҮдёә
$$
\eta = \frac{W}{Q_1}
$$

ејҸдёӯпјҡ$W$жҳҜеҫӘзҺҜиҝҮзЁӢеҜ№еӨ–жүҖеҒҡжҖ»еҠҹпјҢеҚідёә$\triangle ABC$зҡ„йқўз§ҜпјҢжңү
$$
W = \frac{1}{2} p_1 V_1
$$

ејҸдёӯпјҡ$Q_1$жҳҜеҫӘзҺҜиҝҮзЁӢдёӯжҖ»зҡ„еҮҖеҗёзғӯпјҢдёә
$$
Q_1 = Q_{BC} + Q_{CD}
$$

е…¶дёӯ
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{BC} &= C_V (T_C - T_B) = \frac{3}{2} (RT_C - RT_B) \\
&= \frac{3}{2} (2p_1 V_1 - p_1 V_1) = \frac{3}{2} p_1 V_1 \\
Q_{CD} &= \Delta U_{CD} + W_{CD} = C_V (T_D - T_C) + \text{жўҜеҪўйқўз§Ҝ} \\
&= \frac{3}{2} (RT_D - RT_C) + \frac{1}{2} \left(2p_1 + \frac{9}{8} p_1\right) \left(\frac{7}{8} V_1\right) \\
&= \frac{3}{2} \left(\frac{9}{8} p_1 \cdot \frac{15}{8} V_1 - 2p_1 V_1\right) + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{25}{8} p_1 \cdot \frac{7}{8} V_1 \\
&= \frac{21}{128} p_1 V_1 + \frac{175}{128} p_1 V_1 = \frac{49}{32} p_1 V_1
\end{aligned}
$$

дәҺжҳҜ
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_1 &= Q_{BC} + Q_{CD} = \left(\frac{3}{2} + \frac{49}{32}\right) p_1 V_1 = \frac{97}{32} p_1 V_1 \\
\eta &= \frac{W}{Q_1} = \frac{\frac{1}{2} p_1 V_1}{\frac{97}{32} p_1 V_1} = \frac{16}{97} \approx 0.16
\end{aligned}
$$

иҜҒжҳҺеңЁзҗҶжғіж°”дҪ“зҡ„д»»дҪ•еҮҶйқҷжҖҒиҝҮзЁӢдёӯпјҢеҗёзғӯдёҺж”ҫзғӯзҡ„иҪ¬жҠҳзӮ№жҳҜ$p-V$еӣҫдёҠз»қзғӯжӣІзәҝдёҺиҜҘиҝҮзЁӢжӣІзәҝзҡ„еҲҮзӮ№гҖӮ

д»»ж„Ҹе…ғиҝҮзЁӢеҸҜзңӢдҪңеӨҡж–№иҝҮзЁӢгҖӮзғӯеҠӣеӯҰз¬¬дёҖе®ҡеҫӢдёә
$$

$$

е…¶дёӯ
$$
\mathrm{d}U = \frac{M}{\mu} C_V \mathrm{d}T, \quad \mathrm{d}W = p \mathrm{d}V = \frac{M}{\mu} \frac{RT}{V} \mathrm{d}V
$$

еҲ©з”ЁеӨҡж–№иҝҮзЁӢж–№зЁӢ
$$
T V^{n-1} = \text{еёёйҮҸ}, \quad \text{еҫ—} \quad \mathrm{d}W = -\frac{M}{\mu} \frac{R}{n-1} \mathrm{d}T
$$

дәҺжҳҜе…ғиҝҮзЁӢдёӯеҗёзғӯзҡ„е…¬ејҸдёә
$$
\mathrm{d}Q = \frac{M}{\mu} \left(C_V - \frac{R}{n-1}\right) \mathrm{d}T = \frac{M}{\mu} C_V \frac{n - \gamma}{n - 1} \mathrm{d}T
$$

ејҸдёӯпјҡ$n$жҳҜеӨҡж–№жҢҮж•°гҖӮејҸ(5)иЎЁжҳҺ$\mathrm{d}Q$зҡ„жӯЈгҖҒиҙҹеҸ–еҶідәҺ$\mathrm{d}T$еҸҠ$\frac{n - \gamma}{n - 1}$дёӨдёӘеӣ еӯҗгҖӮ

еҰӮзғӯеӣҫ2.19.3жүҖзӨәпјҢз»Ҹ$A$зӮ№дҪңзӯүдҪ“$(n = \infty)$гҖҒз»қзғӯ$(n = \gamma)$гҖҒзӯүжё©$(n = 1)$гҖҒзӯүеҺӢ$(n = 0)$жӣІзәҝпјҢе®ғд»¬жҠҠ$p-V$е№ійқўеҲҶжҲҗе…«дёӘеҢәеҹҹпјҢд»Һ$A$зӮ№еҮәеҸ‘зҡ„д»»ж„Ҹе…ғиҝҮзЁӢеҝ…еңЁе…«еҢәд№ӢдёӯжҲ–жҒ°еңЁиҫ№з•ҢдёҠпјҢ

д»Һ$A \rightarrow$(1), $\gamma < n < \infty$, $\mathrm{d}T > 0$пјҢж•…$\mathrm{d}Q > 0$пјҢеҗёзғӯгҖӮ
д»Һ$A \rightarrow$(2), $n < 0$, $\mathrm{d}T > 0$пјҢж•…$\mathrm{d}Q > 0$пјҢеҗёзғӯгҖӮ
д»Һ$A \rightarrow$(3), $0 < n < 1$, $\mathrm{d}T > 0$пјҢж•…$\mathrm{d}Q > 0$пјҢеҗёзғӯгҖӮ
д»Һ$A \rightarrow$(4), $1 < n < \gamma$, $\mathrm{d}T < 0$пјҢж•…$\mathrm{d}Q > 0$пјҢеҗёзғӯгҖӮ

subject: зү©зҗҶ
raw_question_text: Find the self-inductance $L$ per unit length for an infinitely long coaxial cable of radii $a, b$ with $a<b$, carrying a current $I$, as in the figure. Use energy calculations.

extraction_notes: image entries are PIL object strings rather than file paths or URLs
caption text appears unrelated to the coaxial cable question
metadata.reasoning_chain:
The self-inductance is related to the energy as
\[
W=\frac{L I^{2}}{2}
\]

Let us calculate the energy of the configuration
\[
W=\frac{1}{2 \mu_{o}} \int_{V} B^{2} \mathrm{~d} \tau
\]

Next, we need to find the magnetic field. From AmpГЁre's law, the only significant (non-zero magnetic field region) is for radius $s$ between $a$ and $b$.
\[
\oint \vec{B} \cdot \mathrm{~d} \vec{\ell}=\mu_{o} I_{\mathrm{enc}}
\]
where $I_{\text {enc }}=I$ is the enclosed current. The left-hand side is given by
\[
\oint \vec{B} \cdot \mathrm{~d} \vec{\ell}=B 2 \pi s
\]

From here,
\[
\vec{B}=\frac{\mu_{o} I}{2 \pi s} \hat{\phi}
\]

The energy of a cylindrical shell of length $\ell$, radius $s$ and thickness $\mathrm{d} s$ is:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{d} W & =\frac{1}{2 \mu_{o}} B^{2} 2 \pi \ell s \mathrm{~d} s \\
\mathrm{~d} W & =\frac{1}{2 \mu_{o}}\left(\frac{\mu_{o} I}{2 \pi s}\right)^{2} 2 \pi \ell s \mathrm{~d} s \\
\mathrm{~d} W & =\frac{\mu_{o} I^{2} \ell}{4 \pi} \frac{\mathrm{~d} s}{s}
\end{aligned}
\]

We obtain the total energy by integrating over $s$ from $a$ to $b$
\[
W=\int_{a}^{b} \frac{\mu_{o} I^{2} \ell}{4 \pi} \frac{\mathrm{~d} s}{s}=\left.\frac{\mu_{o} I^{2} \ell}{4 \pi} \ln s\right|_{a} ^{b}=\frac{\mu_{o} I^{2} \ell}{4 \pi} \ln \frac{b}{a}
\]

Back to impedance per unit length:
\[
W=\frac{L I^{2}}{2} \Rightarrow \frac{L}{\ell}=\frac{2 W}{I^{2} \ell}=\frac{\mu_{o}}{2 \pi} \ln \frac{b}{a}
\]

metadata.reasoning_chain:
гҖҗи§ЈгҖ‘еҰӮеҠӣеӣҫ4.3.1жүҖзӨәпјҢиЎҢжҳҹз»•еӣәе®ҡжҒ’жҳҹиҝҗиЎҢзҡ„жҖ»жңәжў°иғҪдёә
$$
E = \frac{1}{2} m v_{0}^{2} - \frac{G m M}{r_{0}}
$$
ејҸдёӯпјҡ$v_{0}$ зҡ„еҸ–еҖјдёҚеҗҢпјҢ$E$ д№ҹдёҚеҗҢпјҢиЎҢжҳҹиҪЁиҝ№зҡ„зұ»еҲ«д№ҹеҗ„ејӮгҖӮ

1. $E = 0$ ж—¶дёәжҠӣзү©зәҝгҖӮжӯӨж—¶иЎҢжҳҹеҲ°иҫҫж— з©·иҝңж—¶йҖҹеәҰеҸҳдёәйӣ¶пјҢеҚіеҲҡеҘҪиғҪйҖғйҖёжҒ’жҳҹзҡ„жқҹзјҡпјҢзӣёеә”зҡ„ $v_{0}$ еҖјз§°дёәйҖғйҖёйҖҹеәҰпјҢз”Ё $v_{\mathrm{e}}$ иЎЁзӨәгҖӮз”ұејҸпјҲ1пјүпјҢ$v_{\mathrm{e}}$ ж»Ўи¶і
$$
\frac{1}{2} m v_{\mathrm{e}}^{2} - \frac{G m M}{r_{0}} = 0, \quad \text{еҚі} \quad v_{\mathrm{e}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 G M}{r_{0}}}
$$

2. $E < 0$ ж—¶дёҖиҲ¬дёәжӨӯеңҶиҪЁйҒ“гҖӮз”ұејҸпјҲ1пјүпјҢ$v_{0}$ еә”ж»Ўи¶і
$$
\frac{1}{2} m v_{0}^{2} - \frac{G m M}{r_{0}} < 0, \quad \text{еҚі} \quad v_{0} < \sqrt{\frac{2 G M}{r_{0}}} = v_{\mathrm{e}}
$$

еңҶиҪЁйҒ“жҳҜжӨӯеңҶиҪЁйҒ“зҡ„зү№ж®Ҡжғ…еҪўгҖӮеңЁжӨӯеңҶиҪЁйҒ“зҡ„жғ…еҪўдёӢпјҢжң¬з« йўҳ1дёӯзҡ„ж–№зЁӢпјҲ10пјүжңүдёӨдёӘе®һж №пјҢеҚі $\rho_{\max}$ е’Ң $\rho_{\min}$ пјҢе®ғд»¬еҜ№еә”дёӨдёӘеҸ–жһҒеҖјзҡ„еҚҠеҫ„ $r_{\min}$ е’Ң $r_{\max}$пјҲеӣ  $\rho = 1 / r$пјүгҖӮз”ұжң¬з« йўҳ1дёӯзҡ„ејҸпјҲ11пјүпјҢжңү
$$
\frac{1}{r_{\min}} + \frac{1}{r_{\max}} = \frac{2 G m^{2} M}{L^{2}}, \quad \text{еҚі} \quad r_{\max} + r_{\min} = r_{\max} r_{\min} \frac{2 G m^{2} M}{L^{2}}
$$

жіЁж„ҸеҲ°пјҢеҰӮеҠӣеӣҫ4.3.2жүҖзӨәпјҢжңү
$$
r_{\max} + r_{\min} = 2 a
$$
ејҸдёӯпјҡ$a$ жҳҜжӨӯеңҶзҡ„еҚҠй•ҝиҪҙпјҢж•…жңү
$$
2 a = r_{\max} r_{\min} \frac{2 G m^{2} M}{L^{2}}
$$

з”ұжң¬з« йўҳ1дёӯзҡ„ејҸпјҲ12пјүпјҢеҚі
$$
\frac{1}{r_{\max} r_{\min}} = -\frac{2 m E}{L^{2}}
$$
ж•…жңү
$$
2 a = -\frac{G m M}{E}
$$

ејҸпјҲ2пјүиЎЁжҳҺпјҢжӨӯеңҶиҪЁйҒ“зҡ„еҚҠй•ҝиҪҙе®Ңе…Ёз”ұжҖ»иғҪйҮҸ $E$ еҶіе®ҡгҖӮе°ҶејҸпјҲ1пјүд»Је…ҘејҸпјҲ2пјүпјҢеҫ—
$$
\frac{1}{2 a} = \frac{1}{r_{0}} - \frac{v_{0}^{2}}{2 G M}
$$

еҪ“жӨӯеңҶйҖҖеҢ–жҲҗеңҶж—¶пјҢжңү
$$
r_{0} = a
$$
е°ҶиЎҢжҳҹеҸ–еңҶиҪЁйҒ“ж—¶зҡ„ $v_{0}$ иЎЁдёә $v_{\mathrm{c}}$ пјҢеҲҷ $v_{\mathrm{c}}$ еә”ж»Ўи¶і
$$
\frac{1}{r_{0}} - \frac{1}{2 r_{0}} = \frac{v_{\mathrm{c}}^{2}}{2 G M}
$$
еҚі
$$
v_{\mathrm{c}}^{2} = \frac{G M}{r_{0}}, \quad v_{\mathrm{c}} = \sqrt{\frac{G M}{r_{0}}} = \frac{v_{\mathrm{e}}}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

3. $E > 0$ ж—¶дёәеҸҢжӣІзәҝгҖӮиЎҢжҳҹе…·жңүжӯЈиғҪйҮҸпјҢж„ҸеҚійҖғйҖёеҲ°ж— з©·иҝңж—¶еҠЁиғҪдёҚдёәйӣ¶гҖӮз”ұејҸпјҲ1пјүпјҢ$v_{0}$ ж»Ўи¶і
$$
\frac{1}{2} m v_{0}^{2} - \frac{G m M}{r_{0}} > 0, \quad \text{еҚі} \quad v_{0} > \sqrt{\frac{2 G M}{r_{0}}} = v_{\mathrm{e}}
$$

жҖ»д№ӢпјҢеҪ“ $v_{0} > v_{\mathrm{e}}$ ж—¶иҪЁйҒ“дёәеҸҢжӣІзәҝпјӣ$v_{0} = v_{\mathrm{e}}$ ж—¶дёәжҠӣзү©зәҝпјӣ$v_{\mathrm{c}} < v_{0} < v_{\mathrm{e}}$ ж—¶дёәпјҲеңЁеңҶиҪЁйҒ“еӨ–йқўзҡ„пјүжӨӯеңҶпјӣ$v_{0} = v_{\mathrm{c}}$ ж—¶дёәеңҶпјӣ$v_{0} < v_{\mathrm{c}}$ ж—¶дёәпјҲеңЁеңҶиҪЁйҒ“йҮҢйқўзҡ„пјүжӨӯеңҶгҖӮд»ҘдёҠеҗ„з§ҚиҪЁйҒ“дёҖе№¶еңЁеҠӣеӣҫ4.3.3дёӯз”»еҮәпјҢд»ҘдҫҝжҜ”иҫғгҖӮ

metadata.reasoning_chain:
еҰӮеүҚжүҖиҝ°пјҢ$t_{0}$ д№ӢеүҚпјҢдәҺ $t$ ж—¶еҲ»д»ҺжЎ¶еә•жјҸеҮәзҡ„ж°ҙеңЁ $t_{0}$ ж—¶еҲ»зҡ„ $x, y$ еқҗж ҮеҲҶеҲ«дёә
$$
\begin{array}{c}
x(t)=\frac{1}{2} a_{0} t_{0}^{2}-\frac{1}{2} a_{0}\left(t_{0}-t\right)^{2}, 
y(t)=\frac{1}{2} g\left(t_{0}-t\right)^{2} .
\end{array}
$$
$t_{0}$ д№ӢеүҚпјҢдәҺ $t+\mathrm{d} t$ ж—¶еҲ»д»ҺжЎ¶еә•жјҸеҮәзҡ„ж°ҙеңЁ $t_{0}$ ж—¶еҲ»зҡ„ $x, y$ еқҗж ҮеҲҶеҲ«дёә
$$
x(t+\mathrm{d} t), y(t+\mathrm{d} t) .
$$
$\mathrm{d} t$ ж—¶й—ҙеҶ…иҙЁйҮҸдёә $\mathrm{d} m=m_{0} \mathrm{~d} t$ зҡ„жјҸж°ҙпјҢеңЁ $t_{0}$ ж—¶еҲ»иҝ№зәҝдёӯеҚ жҚ®зҡ„ $\mathrm{d} x, \mathrm{~d} y$ д»ҘеҸҠй•ҝеәҰ $\mathrm{d} l$ дёә
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{d} x=a_{0}\left(t_{0}-t\right) \mathrm{d} t, \quad \mathrm{~d} y=-g\left(t_{0}-t\right) \mathrm{d} t, 
\mathrm{~d} l=\sqrt{\mathrm{d} x^{2}+\mathrm{d} y^{2}}=\sqrt{a_{0}^{2}+g^{2}}\left(t_{0}-t\right) \mathrm{d} t,
\end{array}
$$

е…¶дёӯ $\mathrm{d} y$ дёәиҙҹпјҢжҳҜеӣ дёә $y(t)>y(t+\mathrm{d} t)$ пјҢеҰӮйўҳи§Јеӣҫ 3 жүҖзӨәгҖӮиҝ№зәҝдёӯ $y(t)$ еӨ„иҙЁйҮҸзәҝеҜҶеәҰдёә
$$
\lambda(t)=\frac{\mathrm{d} m}{\mathrm{~d} l}=m_{0} / \sqrt{a_{0}^{2}+g^{2}}\left(t_{0}-t\right),
$$

е°Ҷ
$$
t_{0}-t=\sqrt{2 y / g}
$$

д»Је…ҘпјҢеҚіеҫ— $t_{0}$ ж—¶еҲ»иҝ№зәҝдёӯиҙЁйҮҸзәҝеҜҶеәҰ $\lambda$ йҡҸ $y$ еқҗж Үзҡ„еҲҶеёғејҸпјҡ
$$
\lambda(y)=\frac{m_{0}}{\sqrt{a_{0}^{2}+g^{2}}} \sqrt{\frac{g}{2 y}} .
$$
